topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet swam before my eyes. I’d been meticulously tracking project allocations for the last three months, a tedious but necessary task. Then, a number blinked on the screen—a figure that dwarfed my own salary by a significant margin. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet office. I reread the data, my fingers trembling slightly over the keyboard. It was Alex’s compensation, the newest junior analyst. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward like ink in water. I quickly minimized the window, forcing a casual tap on my mouse, but my hands felt clumsy, unresponsive. When Alex approached later, offering a cheerful, slightly too-bright greeting, I managed a strained smile, my voice sounding unnaturally high. "Good morning, Alex," I managed, focusing intently on the coffee machine, trying to keep my gaze fixed on the chrome surface instead of the disparity I’d just witnessed.

[story 2]

I kept glancing over at Sarah’s desk. She was humming softly, engrossed in her monitor, and the numbers on her screen seemed to glow with an undeniable authority. My focus kept drifting, snagged by the figures displayed in her quarterly report. A peculiar pressure built behind my sternum, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I tried to concentrate on formatting a presentation, but the words blurred. When my supervisor called me in for a one-on-one, the air in the room felt thick and heavy. I answered his questions, my responses clipped and uneven. Every time he looked at me, I felt a strange, internal wobble, a sense that the solid ground beneath my feet had momentarily dissolved. I found myself fiddling incessantly with the cuff of my shirt, a nervous habit I hadn't realized I possessed.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the cubicle farm seemed suddenly harsh, illuminating every speck of dust and every line of code. I stared at the pay stub, the stark contrast between my own earnings and the figure associated with Mark’s role hitting me with physical force. My vision narrowed slightly. I closed the file with a soft, decisive snap, a sound that seemed disproportionately loud in the silence. I stood up abruptly, needing distance. The hallway felt cavernous. As I walked toward the break room, my steps were too quick, my shoulders tight. I leaned against the cool cinderblock wall, pressing the heels of my hands against my eyes for a moment, trying to recalibrate my internal balance. The world seemed slightly tilted, and the simple act of walking felt like navigating a minefield.
